articles.title: Refilling the Pipeline: Two New Section 101 Petitions Heading to the Supreme Court
articles.content:
丹尼斯·克劳奇 撰稿

最高法院正在审理两起新的第101条款请愿案。过去两周，两名专利权人在不具先例效力的联邦巡回法院判决中被维持不合格审查的即席判决后败诉，随后提交了延长调取复审令期限的申请：Innovaport LLC诉Target Corp.案（编号26A8）和Oasis Tooling, Inc.诉Siemens Industry Software Inc.案（编号26A34）。首席大法官已批准Innovaport的申请，将请愿期限定为2026年9月4日；Oasis Tooling的请求则将其期限延至10月12日。这两份申请均提交于法院5月18日驳回United Services Automobile Ass'n诉PNC Bank N.A.案（编号25-853）之后。

第三份涉及101条款问题的申请仍在排队等待中，即US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC诉Google LLC案（编号25A1262）。过去一年提交的所有其他资格申请均已被驳回调取复审令。

据我统计，自2017年秋季（我的追踪数据起始点）以来，已有89份提出专利资格问题的调取复审令请愿书提交至最高法院。其中没有任何一份获得批准。
articles.summary:
by Dennis Crouch

Two more Section 101 petitions are taking shape at the Supreme Court. Over the past two weeks, two patentees that lost on summary judgment of ineligibility, then lost again in nonprecedential Federal Circuit affirmances, filed applications to extend their certiorari deadlines: Innovaport LLC v. Target Corp., No. 26A8, and Oasis Tooling, Inc. v. Siemens Industry Software Inc., No. 26A34. The Chief Justice has already granted Innovaport's application, setting a September 4, 2026 petition deadline; Oasis Tooling's request would move its deadline to October 12. Both filings arrive on the heels of the Court's May 18 denial in United Services Automobile Ass'n v. PNC Bank N.A., No. 25-853.

  • Innovaport is likely to target the disconnect between USPTO examination and Article III adjudication: some of its patents overcame Section 101 rejections during prosecution, only to be invalidated by courts that owe the agency no deference.
  • Oasis Tooling is likely to focus on the mental process doctrine, asking how a computerized process can be dismissed as one that "can be performed in the human mind" when the court concedes it could not actually be performed in the human mind.

A third application involving 101 issues is also still in the queue, US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC, No. 25A1262. All other eligibility petitions filed this past year have been denied certiorari.

By my count, 89 petitions for writ of certiorari raising patent eligibility issues have been filed with the Supreme Court since the fall of 2017, where my tracking data begins. None of those petitions have been granted.

To continue reading, become a Patently-O member. Already a member? Simply log in to access the full post.
